does: This paper introduces EditReward, a learned reward model designed to evaluate instruction-guided image editing by training on a large-scale human preference dataset of over 200K expert-annotated pairs. The model achieves state-of-the-art performance in aligning with human judgments across multiple benchmarks including GenAI-Bench, AURORA-Bench, ImagenHub, and a newly introduced EditReward-Bench. Beyond evaluation, the authors demonstrate EditReward's practical utility by using it to filter the noisy ShareGPT-4o-Image dataset and select high-quality training data for developing Step1X-Edit, an improved open-source instruction-guided image editing model. The approach systematically addresses the reliability bottleneck in training synthetic data for image editing tasks.
connects: This work builds on recent advances in instruction-guided image generation and editing, where models like GPT-Image-1, Seedream, and Google-Nano-Banana have shown strong results but remain proprietary. The paper directly responds to the open-source lagging problem by adopting reward model approaches from reinforcement learning from human feedback, a paradigm that has proven effective in aligning language models and has begun seeing application in multimodal domains. The work extends vision-language model evaluation approaches by developing a task-specific reward model rather than relying on general VLM-as-judge baselines, building on recent recognition that domain-specific reward modeling outperforms generic evaluation metrics. The reliance on carefully annotated human preference data reflects established best practices from RLHF literature adapted to the image editing context.
enables: EditReward establishes a foundation for scaling high-quality instruction-guided image editing through data curation and model improvement pipelines. By providing a reliable reward signal, it enables future work to apply reinforcement learning directly to edit generation models, potentially bridging the gap between open-source and proprietary systems. The large-scale preference dataset and EditReward-Bench create standardized evaluation infrastructure for the community, facilitating systematic comparison of editing approaches. The demonstrated effectiveness of reward-based data filtering suggests broader applications in improving noisy multimodal datasets across generation tasks. Future research could leverage EditReward for iterative model improvement through online learning, explore its transfer to related editing domains, or develop refinements that capture more nuanced editing quality dimensions beyond current preference-based annotations.
